content.verdict: Format and Pixpa are both trial-based portfolio builders for photographers and creatives with client galleries and commission-free stores, but they split on where the commission-free promise kicks in and how many templates you get to start from. Pixpa keeps zero commission on every plan from $5.40/month and ships 200+ templates plus a 30-day money-back guarantee, while Format holds its commission-free store back for the Pro plan and above but adds hosted video and Weglot-powered multilingual sites that Pixpa's facts don't mention.
content.bestForToolA: Format fits creatives who want their store fee-free from day one on a mid-tier plan and who need extras like up to 120 minutes of hosted video (Pro Plus) or a multilingual site via the built-in Weglot integration.
content.bestForToolB: Pixpa fits photographers who want a zero-commission store on the cheapest plan available, a larger template library (200+) to start from, and a 30-day money-back guarantee plus a longer 15-day trial before committing.
content.whoNeedsBoth: Agencies or studios evaluating portfolio platforms for multiple photographer clients may end up testing both trials side by side, since each offers a different balance of store economics (Format's Basic plan has no store at all, while Pixpa is commission-free from its very first plan) and gallery/proofing depth.

keyDifferences.title: Commission-free store availability
keyDifferences.toolA: Format's commission-free store is only available on the Pro plan and above; the entry-level Basic plan has no online store at all.
keyDifferences.toolB: Pixpa's store carries zero commission on every plan, including the entry-level Basic plan at $5.40/month.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Sellers on a budget need to know whether they must upgrade tiers just to sell without a cut taken from each sale.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Photographers and creatives who plan to sell prints or digital files from day one on the cheapest plan.

keyDifferences.title: Template and theme library size
keyDifferences.toolA: Format offers 90+ themes built specifically for photography and creative portfolios.
keyDifferences.toolB: Pixpa offers 200+ designer-made, responsive templates customizable with layout, style, and custom CSS.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: A larger starting template library gives more starting points before customization is needed.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Users who want to launch quickly with a template close to their desired look without heavy customization.

keyDifferences.title: Trial length and refund policy
keyDifferences.toolA: Format offers a 14-day free trial with no credit card required, with no money-back guarantee mentioned in the facts.
keyDifferences.toolB: Pixpa offers a 15-day free trial with no credit card required, plus a 30-day money-back guarantee on top of the trial.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: A longer evaluation window plus a refund guarantee reduces the financial risk of committing to a paid plan.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Buyers who want extra downside protection after moving from trial to paid plan.

keyDifferences.title: Hosted video and multilingual support
keyDifferences.toolA: Format supports up to 120 minutes of hosted video on higher plans and multilingual sites via a built-in Weglot integration.
keyDifferences.toolB: Pixpa's facts do not document hosted video or a site-translation feature for published sites.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Video hosting and multilingual publishing are relevant to creatives who show motion work or serve non-English-speaking clients.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Filmmakers or creatives who need embedded video and photographers with international clientele.

keyDifferences.title: Client gallery packaging
keyDifferences.toolA: Format includes client galleries directly within its main plans, scaling from 3 galleries on Basic up to 250 on Pro Plus.
keyDifferences.toolB: Pixpa includes client galleries in its main plans but also sells a Standalone Client Gallery plan ($5/month) as a separate add-on for proofing-only needs.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: A standalone gallery-only option can be cheaper for users who only need proofing without a full site builder.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Photographers who just need client proofing and delivery without building a full portfolio site.

rows.note: Format's Basic plan has no store; commission-free store starts at Pro
Format: up to 120 minutes on Pro Plus
Format: 14 days; Pixpa: 15 days, both no credit card
Neither tool's facts mention AI features

faqs.question: Does Format or Pixpa charge commission on store sales?
faqs.answer: Pixpa is commission-free on every plan, including its $5.40/month entry tier. Format's store is also commission-free, but only from the Pro plan upward — its Basic plan doesn't include a store at all.

faqs.question: Which one has more templates to start from?
faqs.answer: Pixpa lists 200+ designer-made templates, while Format offers 90+ themes built specifically for photography and creative portfolios.

faqs.question: Do Format and Pixpa offer free trials?
faqs.answer: Yes, both offer no-credit-card trials — Format's runs 14 days, and Pixpa's runs 15 days. Pixpa also adds a 30-day money-back guarantee after that.

faqs.question: Can I host video with either platform?
faqs.answer: Format supports up to 120 minutes of hosted video on its Pro Plus plan. Pixpa's facts don't document a video hosting feature.

faqs.question: Is there a way to get just client proofing galleries without a full site builder?
faqs.answer: Pixpa sells a Standalone Client Gallery plan for $5/month that provides proofing-only functionality. Format's client galleries are bundled into its main plans rather than offered standalone.

faqs.question: Do either of these tools include AI features?
faqs.answer: No — the facts for both Format and Pixpa explicitly note that no AI features are mentioned on either site.

content.verdict: Typedream and Ucraft are both freemium no-code builders, but they're built around different core jobs: Typedream bundles a website builder with link-in-bio pages and digital product sales (ebooks, courses, files) under a beehiiv-owned brand, while Ucraft centers on a wider e-commerce ladder — from a 15-page free site up to a $75/month Unlimited plan with POS and tax/VAT tools — plus a built-in AI support assistant called Hoory that Typedream's facts don't mention. Neither is a strict upgrade of the other; the right pick depends on whether you're selling digital content and building an audience, or running a store that needs to scale into physical-product logistics.
content.bestForToolA: Typedream fits creators who want to combine a website, a link-in-bio page, a CMS-backed blog, and digital product sales (ebooks, courses, templates, files) in one place, and who are comfortable with a 2% transaction fee on paid plans.
content.bestForToolB: Ucraft fits users building an online store that needs to grow — from a free 15-page site through tiers with 0% transaction fees, abandoned cart recovery, 50+ payment methods, and eventually POS and VAT handling, plus a built-in AI assistant (Hoory) for customer support.
content.whoNeedsBoth: A creator selling both digital products (courses, ebooks) and physical merchandise might use Typedream for the audience-facing blog and link-in-bio funnel while running a Ucraft storefront for physical inventory, shipping, and abandoned-cart recovery.

keyDifferences.title: Built-in AI assistant
keyDifferences.toolA: Typedream's facts do not mention any AI features.
keyDifferences.toolB: Ucraft includes Hoory, a built-in AI assistant for evaluating customer experience and setting up automated support.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: An included AI support tool can reduce the need for a separate customer-service integration.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Store owners who want automated customer support without adding another tool.

keyDifferences.title: Free plan scope
keyDifferences.toolA: Typedream's free plan is limited to a single published page and takes a 5% transaction fee on sales.
keyDifferences.toolB: Ucraft's Free Website plan supports up to 15 pages with unlimited bandwidth, though it carries Ucraft branding.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: The free tier determines how much can be built and tested before paying anything.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Users testing the builder before committing who need more than a single page.

keyDifferences.title: E-commerce depth and transaction fees
keyDifferences.toolA: Typedream charges a 2% transaction fee (plus payment processing fees) on paid plans for selling digital products.
keyDifferences.toolB: Ucraft reaches 0% transaction fees starting at its $13/month Basic Shop plan, and adds abandoned cart recovery on Pro Shop and above plus POS integration and advanced tax/VAT management on the $75/month Unlimited plan.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Transaction fees compound with sales volume, and dedicated commerce features (cart recovery, POS, tax tools) matter more as a store scales.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Merchants running a growing physical or mixed-product store rather than occasional digital sales.

keyDifferences.title: Content publishing model
keyDifferences.toolA: Typedream includes CMS-backed blog collections — 1 collection on the Launch plan, unlimited on Grow — alongside a link-in-bio page.
keyDifferences.toolB: Ucraft's Website plan lists blogging as an included feature but does not document CMS collection depth in the same way.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: CMS collection limits affect how much structured blog content can be organized and published.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Creators publishing a high volume of blog content who need collection-based organization.

keyDifferences.title: Company backing and continuity
keyDifferences.toolA: Typedream now operates as a beehiiv-owned product rather than an independent company (acquired June 2024), per its own FAQ.
keyDifferences.toolB: Ucraft's facts do not mention any acquisition or ownership change.
keyDifferences.whyItMatters: Ownership changes can affect roadmap priorities and long-term product direction.
keyDifferences.benefitsWho: Buyers evaluating platform stability before committing to a paid annual plan.

rows.note: Typedream: 1 collection on Launch, unlimited on Grow; Ucraft: blogging listed on Website plan
Ucraft: from Basic Shop ($13/mo) and above; Typedream charges 2% on all paid plans
Ucraft: Pro Shop plan and above
Ucraft: 14-day free trial on paid plans
Typedream: 14-day money-back guarantee on paid plans
Typedream: free 1-year .xyz domain on paid plans

faqs.question: Does either Typedream or Ucraft include a built-in AI assistant?
faqs.answer: Ucraft includes Hoory, a built-in AI assistant for evaluating customer experience and automating support. Typedream's facts do not mention any AI features.

faqs.question: How do the free plans compare?
faqs.answer: Typedream's free plan is limited to a single page with a 5% transaction fee on sales. Ucraft's Free Website plan allows up to 15 pages with unlimited bandwidth, though it displays Ucraft branding.

faqs.question: Which one is cheaper to start with online selling?
faqs.answer: Ucraft reaches 0% transaction fees at its $13/month Basic Shop plan. Typedream charges a 2% transaction fee on all of its paid plans (starting at $15/month annually), on top of payment processing fees.

faqs.question: Can I sell digital products like ebooks or courses?
faqs.answer: Yes — Typedream is built around selling ebooks, courses, templates, files, and services directly from the site. Ucraft's facts focus on general e-commerce with payment and shipping methods rather than digital-product-specific tools.

faqs.question: Is Typedream still independently run?
faqs.answer: No — per Typedream's own FAQ, it was acquired by beehiiv in June 2024 and continues to operate under the Typedream name.

faqs.question: Do either of these offer a trial or money-back guarantee?
faqs.answer: Ucraft offers a 14-day free trial on paid plans with no credit card required. Typedream instead offers a 14-day money-back guarantee on its paid plans, plus a free 1-year .xyz domain.
